content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

build in publicBuilding an app is exciting. Shipping it feels like crossing the finish line.
But then reality hits.
No users.
No signups.
No feedback.
No growth.
The problem usually isn’t that your app is bad. It’s that nobody knows it exists.
Instead of spending months adding more features, follow this process.
Step 1: Define One Specific User
If your answer to “Who is this for?” is “everyone,” you’ve already lost.
Write down one sentence:
“This app helps [specific person] solve [specific problem].”
The narrower your audience, the easier it becomes to find them.

Step 2: Talk to Real People
Don’t guess.
Find 20–50 people who match your target audience.
You can find them on:

• Reddit

• LinkedIn

• X (Twitter)

• Discord communities

• Facebook Groups

• Slack communities

• Product Hunt discussions
Don’t pitch your app immediately.
Ask questions like:

• How are you solving this problem today?

• What’s the hardest part?

• What’s frustrating about current solutions?

• Have you paid for a solution before?
You’re looking for patterns, not compliments.

Step 3: Make Your App Solve One Problem Extremely Well
Most early products try to do too much.
Your first version should solve one painful problem better than anyone else.
Ask yourself:
If someone only used one feature, would they still recommend the app?
If the answer is no, simplify.

Step 4: Write a Message People Instantly Understand
Most landing pages are confusing.
Someone should understand your app in less than five seconds.
Use this structure:
We help [who] achieve [result] without [big frustration].
Avoid technical jargon.
Talk about outcomes, not features. .. people want change, transformation infuse that in your narrative, the different identity they will have in future while using the app, annoucing features and benefits are extra layers to drive the first.

Step 5: Pick One Growth Channel
Don’t try SEO, TikTok, YouTube, Instagram, LinkedIn, Reddit, newsletters, paid ads, influencers, and email marketing all at once.
Choose one.
For example:

• B2B → LinkedIn

• Developers → Reddit or Hacker News

• Designers → X and design communities

• Consumers → TikTok or Instagram

• Founders → Product Hunt and Indie Hackers
So now if you get the idea correctly, when you understand your users and know who they are, they will inform what channel to go with.
One channel mastered beats ten channels ignored.

Step 6: Launch Where Your Users Already Spend Time
Don’t wait for people to magically discover your website.
Go where they already are.
Share:

• lessons learned

• behind-the-scenes updates

• customer stories

• quick tutorials

• useful insights related to the problem you solve
Become valuable before becoming promotional.
People trust creators who consistently teach.

Step 7: Measure What Actually Matters
You don’t need dozens of analytics dashboards.
Track only these numbers:

• Website visitors

• Sign-up rate

• Activation rate (people who experience value)

• Weekly active users

• Retention

• Referrals
Useful tools include:

• Google Analytics

• PostHog

• Mixpanel

• Microsoft Clarity
If you don’t measure, you’re guessing.

Step 8: Fix Leaks Before Adding More Traffic
Imagine pouring water into a bucket with holes.
That’s what marketing feels like when users don’t stay.
If people leave after signing up, don’t spend more money acquiring users.
Fix:

• onboarding

• user experience

• loading speed

• confusing flows

• missing value
Retention creates growth.

Step 9: Build in Public
People love following progress.
Share:

• product updates

• failures

• experiments

• user feedback

• milestones

• metrics

• what you’re learning
Consistency creates familiarity.
Familiarity builds trust.
Trust brings users.

Step 10: Scale Only After People Keep Coming Back
Many founders think they have a marketing problem.
Most actually have a retention problem.
Before spending on ads or hiring a growth team, ask:

• Are users returning?

• Are they inviting others?

• Would they be disappointed if the app disappeared tomorrow?
If the answer is yes, you’ve earned the right to scale.
If not, keep improving the product.

Useful Resources
For finding users:

• Reddit

• LinkedIn

• Discord

• Facebook Groups

• X (Twitter)

• Indie Hackers
For launching:

• Product Hunt

• Hacker News

• BetaList

• Uneed

• Dev Hunt
For analytics:

• Google Analytics

• PostHog

• Mixpanel

• Microsoft Clarity
For email collection:

• Beehiiv

• Kit (formerly ConvertKit)

• Mailchimp
For scheduling content:

• Buffer

• Hypefury

• Typefully
For design feedback:

• Dribbble

• Behance

• UX communities

• Design Buddies

The Formula
Most successful apps don’t win because they launch.
They win because they repeat this cycle relentlessly:
Find users → Listen → Improve → Share → Measure → Repeat.
Building the product is only the beginning.
Distribution is the second product you have to build.
